en.title: AI Documentation Workflow
en.content:
AI Documentation Workflow

Roles and collaboration model

Codex (primary documentation builder)

- Scans repository and verifies technical claims against source files.
- Builds and maintains docs architecture in .
- Produces comprehensive, structured technical pages.

Copilot (translation and small sections)

- Translates English source into Spanish and Basque.
- Handles small targeted expansions and formatting refinements.
- Should not modify core technical claims without verification.

Claude (review and editorial QA)

- Reviews correctness, coherence, and readability.
- Flags gaps, contradictions, and missing references.
- Recommends structural improvements for maintainability.

Non-negotiable rules

- No AI agent should invent system behavior.
- All uncertain areas must be explicitly marked as Needs verification.
- Every technical statement should be traceable to current repository code.

Suggested handoff sequence

1. Codex writes/updates English source pages.
2. Claude reviews and requests corrections.
3. Copilot translates dictionaries/content once source is stable.
4. Codex performs final consistency pass and publishes.

Translation next steps (for Copilot)

- Translate all strings in .
- Translate all strings in .
- Optionally split content files by locale once internationalized routing is introduced.

en.title: Auth
en.content:
Auth

Base URL:  · Port 3001 in development.

Auth endpoints handle login/logout, JWT token lifecycle, password reset, and new-account activation. Most are public (no Bearer token required). The access token is a short-lived JWT (default 15 min); the refresh token is a long-lived UUID stored in the  table.

---

Token flow

---

POST /api/auth/login

Auth required: No

Request body

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
|  | string | ✓ | Valid email address |
|  | string | ✓ | User's plaintext password |

Response 200

Sets . Lifetime controlled by .

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 400 |  | Missing or invalid email |
| 400 |  | Missing password |
| 401 |  | Wrong email or password |
| 403 |  | Account exists but  |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

> Side effect: Inserts a refresh token row in . Uses a dummy bcrypt comparison when user is not found to prevent timing attacks.

---

POST /api/auth/refresh

Auth required: No (cookie-based)

Rotates the refresh token. The old token is revoked; a new one is issued and set as a cookie. Returns a fresh access token in the body.

Request: No body.  cookie is read automatically.

Response 200

Sets a new .

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 400 |  | Cookie missing or empty |
| 401 |  | Token not in DB |
| 401 |  | Token already revoked |
| 401 |  | TTL exceeded |
| 403 |  | User linked to token is inactive |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

> Side effect: Revokes old token, inserts new token in  table.

---

POST /api/auth/logout

Auth required: Yes —  or  (Bearer token)

Revokes the current refresh token and clears the cookie.

Request: No body.

Response 200

Sets .

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 401 |  | No  header |
| 401 |  | Malformed or expired access token |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

> Side effect: Marks the refresh token as revoked in the  table.

---

GET /api/auth/me

Auth required: Yes —  or  (Bearer token)

Returns the profile of the currently authenticated user.

Request: No body.

Response 200

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 401 |  | No  header |
| 401 |  | Malformed or expired access token |
| 404 |  | User in token no longer exists in DB |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

---

POST /api/auth/forgotPassword

Auth required: No

Sends a password-reset email if the address is registered. Always returns the same response to prevent email enumeration.

Request body

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
|  | string | ✓ | Email address to send the reset link to |

Response 200

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 400 |  | Invalid or missing email |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

> Side effect (if email exists): Revokes existing reset tokens for the user, inserts a new , sends a password-reset email. Token expiry controlled by .

---

PATCH /api/auth/changePwd

Auth required: No (token-based via request body)

Dual-mode endpoint. With only  it validates the token (useful for showing an error page on invalid links). With both fields it sets the new password.

Request body

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
|  | string | ✓ | One-time token from reset/activation email |
|  | string | ✗ | Min 6 characters. If omitted, only validates the token. |

Mode 1 — token check only

Response 200: 

Mode 2 — set new password

Response 200: 

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 400 |  | Missing token |
| 400 |  | Too-short password |
| 401 |  | Token not found in DB or already used |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

> Side effect (password change): Hashes and saves the new password. Sets . Revokes all reset tokens for the user.

---

POST /api/auth/activateAccount

Auth required: No (activation token required)

Activates a newly created user account. Called when the user follows the activation link from their email.

Request body

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
|  | string | ✓ | Email of the account to activate |

> [!WARNING] Needs verification
> The exact request shape (body fields vs query params) was not confirmed from a  file — the  folder has no . This endpoint is invoked internally by ; the public-facing handler may not exist yet or may share the  flow via .

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 401 |  | Token unknown, revoked, or expired |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

API Reference

The pakAG backend is a Next.js 15 App Router API. All endpoints live under  and run on port 3001 in development.

Base URL

Authentication

All endpoints require  except where noted as Auth required: No.

Obtain an access token via [](/en/api/auth#post-apiauthlogin). Tokens expire after 15 minutes (default). Refresh silently with [](/en/api/auth#post-apiauthrefresh), which rotates the refresh token cookie.

Roles:  |

Error format

Every error response uses a consistent envelope:

HTTP status codes:

| Code | Meaning                                       |
| ---- | --------------------------------------------- |
| 400  | Validation error                              |
| 401  | Unauthenticated (missing or invalid token)    |
| 403  | Forbidden (wrong role, or resource not owned) |
| 404  | Resource not found                            |
| 409  | Conflict (duplicate, FK constraint)           |

Pagination

Paginated endpoints accept  (default ) and  (default , max ) as query parameters and return:

The field name for the array varies per resource (, , ).

| Auth         |      | Login, logout, token refresh, password reset, account activation     |

Package status lifecycle

Logs

Base URL:  · Port 3001 in development.

Every time a package status changes, a log entry is inserted into the  table. This resource exposes read-only access to those entries for admin auditing. Distributors can query logs only for packages assigned to them.

---

GET /api/logs/listAll

Auth required: Yes —  only

Returns a paginated log of all package status changes across the system, with optional filters.

Query parameters

| Param | Type | Default | Description |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|
|  | number | — | Filter by package ID (must exist) |
|  | number | — | Filter by user ID who made the change |
|  | string | — | Start date filter (any valid date string) |
|  | string | — | End date filter (any valid date string) |
|  | number |  | Page number |
|  | number |  | Results per page (1–100) |

Response 200

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|

| 400 |  | Non-existent user |
| 400 |  | Unparseable date |
| 400 |  | Unparseable date |
| 401 |  | No token |
| 403 |  | Caller is not  |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

en.content:
Packages

Base URL:  · Port 3001 in development.

Packages are the core entity of pakAG. Each package has a delivery address (geocoded via Google Maps), a tracking token, and a lifecycle of statuses. Admin endpoints manage the full package lifecycle; distributors interact with packages assigned to them.

Package statuses:  |  |  |  |  | 

Status transitions (distributor-enforced):
- 
- 
- 

Each transition triggers a status log entry and a tracking email to the recipient.

---

POST /api/packages/create

Auth required: Yes —  only

Creates a package. Geocodes the delivery address via Google Maps, generates a tracking token, logs the creation, and sends a tracking email to the recipient.

Request body

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
|  | string | ✓ | Name of the recipient |
|  | string | ✓ | Email of the recipient |
|  | number \| null | ✓ | Distributor user ID, or  |

en.title: Routes
en.content:
Routes

Base URL:  · Port 3001 in development.

Routes represent an ordered set of delivery stops for a distributor on a specific date. Route creation calls the Google Directions API to optimize stop order (TSP) and compute estimated arrival times.

Route statuses:  |  | 

---

POST /api/routes/create

Auth required: Yes —  only

Creates an optimized delivery route for a distributor on a given future date. Packages must already be  to the target distributor. The service also includes any "carryover" packages (packages still  from previous days) up to a maximum of 20 stops total.

Request body

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
|  | number | ✓ | Distributor user ID (must exist and be  role) |
|  | string | ✓ | Route date in  format (must be at least tomorrow) |
|  | number[] | ✓ | Non-empty array of package IDs (no duplicates; all must be  to ) |

Response 201

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |

| 400 |  | Missing or invalid  |
| 400 |  | Invalid date format |
| 400 |  | Date is today or in the past |
| 400 |  | Empty or duplicate IDs |
| 401 |  | No token |
| 403 |  | Caller is not  |
| 404 |  |  does not exist |

en.title: Tracking
en.content:
Tracking

Base URL:  · Port 3001 in development.

The tracking endpoint is public — no authentication required. Each package is assigned a 32-byte hex tracking token at creation time. The token is emailed to the recipient so they can follow their delivery status without logging in.

Tokens expire after  days (default: 30). An expired or unknown token returns .

---

GET /api/tracking/[trackingToken]

Auth required: No

Returns the current status and address of a package identified by its opaque tracking token.

Path parameter

| Param | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
|  | string | ✓ | 32-byte hex tracking token from the recipient's email |

Response 200

Errors

| Status | Message | Condition |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 404 |  | Token is unknown, revoked, or expired |
| 500 |  | Unhandled exception |

> Note: The response deliberately exposes no personal information beyond the recipient name and delivery address — both are already known to the recipient. No email address or user IDs are returned.

en.title: Architecture Decisions
en.content:
Architecture Decisions

This page documents the key architectural choices made in pakAG using an ADR (Architecture Decision Record) format. Each entry explains the context, decision, rationale, and tradeoffs accepted.

---

ADR-001 — Next.js Route Handlers instead of Express (or Fastify)

Context

pakAG needed a REST API with JSON responses, JWT auth, and HTTP-only cookie management. The classic Node.js choice would have been an Express or Fastify server in a dedicated package.

Decision

Use Next.js 16 App Router Route Handlers ( files exporting , , etc.) as the API server.

Rationale

1. Team already uses Next.js for the frontend. Choosing the same framework for the backend means the team writes TypeScript with the same toolchain, same  conventions, and same deployment model.
2. No custom server required. Express requires a  entrypoint, process management, and port binding. Next.js handles all of that with  / .
3. Web API compatibility. Route Handlers use the standard  /  types (not Express's  / ). This makes the code closer to web standards and portable to edge runtimes.
4. Zero extra dependencies. No , , , or routing library to install, version, or audit.
5. Middleware via . If global concerns (rate limiting, CORS headers) are needed later, Next.js middleware covers them without additional packages.

Tradeoffs accepted

| Tradeoff | Impact |
|---|---|
| Next.js is a heavier runtime than bare Express | Higher cold-start memory; acceptable for an always-on deployment |
| No  middleware chain | Auth is called manually in each ; mitigated by the  helper |
| Next.js opinionates the file structure | Cannot place route files arbitrarily — must follow  |
| React and react-dom are installed even though the backend renders no UI | Dead weight; ~3 MB; acceptable for simplicity |

---

ADR-002 — mysql2 directly instead of an ORM (Prisma, TypeORM, Drizzle)

Context

The project needs to read and write to a MySQL 8 database. Modern TypeScript projects often choose Prisma or Drizzle for type-safe queries.

Decision

Use mysql2 with raw SQL prepared statements. No ORM or query builder.

Rationale

1. Complete control over queries. The route optimization feature requires complex joins, subqueries, and MySQL-specific functions (e.g., date arithmetic). ORMs abstract these away in ways that frequently require raw query escapes anyway.
2. No migration layer needed.  is the source of truth. The DB schema is managed directly, not through ORM migration files that need to stay in sync with the schema.
3. Explicit SQL is readable. Developers reading  files see the exact query, the exact columns, and the exact indexes used. There is no intermediary that generates a different query than expected.
4. No code generation step. Prisma requires  to regenerate the client after schema changes. mysql2 requires nothing beyond running the updated .
5. Performance predictability. Prepared statements are compiled by MySQL once and reused. No ORM query planner surprises.
6. Bundle size. Prisma's generated client is several megabytes. mysql2 is under 500 KB.

Tradeoffs accepted

| Tradeoff | Impact |
|---|---|
| No compile-time query type inference | Repository return types must be typed manually |
| Developers must know SQL | Cannot hide SQL behind an abstraction layer |
|  casting is manual | Each repository must cast results to domain types |
| No automatic relation loading | Joins must be written explicitly |

> [!NOTE]
> The typing gap is partially mitigated by the strict architectural rule: repositories always return domain types, never raw . This creates a typed boundary at the repository/service interface even if the SQL itself is untyped.

---

ADR-003 — JWT (stateless) instead of server-side sessions

Context

The API needs to authenticate requests from the frontend (same origin during development, potentially cross-origin in production). Options considered: session cookies backed by DB or Redis, or stateless JWTs.

Decision

Use JWT access tokens (HS256, 15-minute TTL) delivered as  headers, paired with opaque refresh tokens stored in the database and delivered as HttpOnly cookies.

Rationale

1. Stateless access tokens. The API can verify any access token by checking its signature and TTL without a DB lookup. This means every request other than refresh is pure CPU work — no extra roundtrip to a session store.
2. Short-lived access tokens limit the blast radius. If a token is stolen, it expires in 15 minutes. There is no way to invalidate individual access tokens (no revocation list), so keeping them short-lived is the key mitigation.
3. Refresh token rotation in DB. Refresh tokens are stored in the  table. Each  call deletes the old token and inserts a new one. This gives us revocability for long-lived sessions (logout, suspicious activity) without a Redis dependency.
4. No session store infrastructure. Sessions require a shared store (Redis, Memcached, or DB table with tight index) that must stay available. JWTs move this complexity to the client.
5. Standard pattern for SPAs. The frontend is an SPA;  headers are the conventional approach for API-to-SPA auth.

Tradeoffs accepted

| Tradeoff | Impact |
|---|---|
| Access tokens cannot be revoked mid-life | A stolen token is valid until expiry (15 min max) |
| JWT payload is visible to anyone (only signed, not encrypted) | Do not put sensitive data in the payload — only  and  |
| Refresh token storage adds a DB dependency | Every  hits the  table |
| Two tokens to manage | Frontend must handle both; mitigated by Axios interceptors |

> [!WARNING]
> The current implementation has a known security issue:  has a hardcoded fallback in . In production,  must be set as an environment variable and the fallback must be removed.

---

ADR-004 — pnpm workspaces without Nx or Turborepo

Context

The project has three packages that need to be developed and run together. Monorepo tooling options include Nx, Turborepo, Lerna, or plain workspace managers.

Decision

Use pnpm workspaces with a minimal . No build orchestrator (no Nx, no Turborepo).

Rationale

1. Three packages only. Nx and Turborepo solve build graph caching and parallel execution for projects with tens or hundreds of packages. With three packages, the overhead is not justified.
2. No shared build artifacts. , , and  do not share compiled output. There is no need to track whether package A needs to be rebuilt before package B.
3. Simple developer experience.  at the root installs everything. Three root scripts (, , ) launch each package with . No config files, no cache directories, no plugin system to learn.
4. Faster onboarding. A new developer can understand the entire monorepo setup by reading 4 lines of  and 9 lines of . Nx/Turborepo configs routinely run to hundreds of lines.
5. pnpm's native workspace protocol. The  version specifier (used by  to depend on ) is a first-class pnpm feature that requires no extra tooling.

Tradeoffs accepted

| Tradeoff | Impact |
|---|---|
| No incremental build caching | All three packages are rebuilt from scratch every time |
| No dependency graph visualization | Not needed at this scale |
| Root scripts must be extended manually for each new package | Low friction at three packages; would need tooling at 10+ |

---

ADR-005 — No  package for cross-cutting code

Context

In many monorepos, a  or  package exports types, utilities, and components used by multiple other packages. pakAG was evaluated for whether such a shared package is needed.

Decision

Do not create a  package. Each package owns its types, utilities, and components in full.

Rationale

1. Backend and frontend have no shared types. The backend is an API server; the frontend is a client. They communicate over HTTP/JSON. There are no TypeScript types that both need to compile against — the contract is the HTTP API, not shared source code.
2. Shared code creates coupling. If  contains a type used by both backend and frontend, changing that type requires updating both packages and testing both in the same PR. This defeats one of the main benefits of a monorepo (independent deployability).
3. The three packages run in different runtimes.  runs in Node.js and uses mysql2, bcrypt, jsonwebtoken.  runs in the browser and uses React, Axios, Tailwind. There are very few utilities that make sense in both environments.
4.  dependency on  is a documented exception.  imports  via  to reuse UI primitives in documentation examples. This is acceptable because it is build-time only and documented.
5. Avoiding premature abstraction. Creating a shared package before a concrete need is identified leads to an anemic abstraction that either grows uncontrolled or remains empty.

Tradeoffs accepted

| Tradeoff | Impact |
|---|---|
| Some type definitions may be duplicated | Acceptable — HTTP DTOs are intentionally decoupled from DB schemas |
| No shared ESLint / TypeScript config package | Each package maintains its own config; consistent by convention |

> [!TIP]
> If a genuine cross-cutting need emerges (e.g., shared validation rules used by both frontend form validation and backend DTO validation), the correct response is to evaluate it case by case — not to preemptively create a shared package.

---

ADR-006 — No Zod (or other runtime validation library) in DTOs

Context

TypeScript's type system is compile-time only. At runtime, an API request body is . Popular approaches to runtime validation include Zod, Yup, class-validator, and custom guards.

Decision

Use custom type guard functions from  (, , , etc.) instead of a schema validation library like Zod.

Rationale

1. Minimal bundle impact. Zod is ~13 KB gzipped. In a server-only context (Route Handlers do not ship to the browser), this is not a critical concern, but keeping zero extra dependencies avoids supply chain risk.
2. Explicit error messages. Each  statement produces an error message written by the developer, not generated by a library. Validation messages are consistent with the rest of the error surface.
3. No schema duplication. With Zod, developers write a schema (Zod shape) and a TypeScript interface, and then either use  or write both manually. The custom guard approach writes the interface once and guards match it.
4. Easier to read for newcomers. The DTO validation logic is plain TypeScript  statements — no DSL to learn.

Tradeoffs accepted

| Tradeoff | Impact |
|---|---|
| More boilerplate per endpoint | Each DTO requires manually written guards |
| No automatic type inference from schema | The TypeScript interface must be kept in sync with the guards manually |
| No nested object validation helpers | Complex nested DTOs require nested guard composition |

en.title: Architecture
en.content:
Architecture

pakAG is a pnpm monorepo composed of three independently deployed packages. Each package has a single, well-defined responsibility. They share no source code at runtime — the only shared resource is the MySQL database.

Package map

| Package | Framework | Language | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
|  | Next.js 16 App Router | TypeScript | API server (Route Handlers only) |
|  | Next.js 16 App Router | TypeScript + Tailwind CSS 4 | SPA-style admin / distributor UI |
|  | Next.js + Nextra 4 | MDX | Developer documentation |

System context diagram

Package responsibilities

— the API

 is a pure API server. It uses Next.js only for its Route Handler infrastructure; no React pages or UI components are rendered. Every file under  exports HTTP verb functions (, , , ).

Key responsibilities:
- Authentication and authorization (JWT HS256, role enforcement)
- Package lifecycle management (create → assign → transit → deliver)
- Route optimization (Google Directions API + stop ordering)
- Email notifications (Resend via transactional templates)
- Public package tracking (token-based, no auth required)
- MySQL connection pooling via 

— the UI

 is a Next.js application that acts as a Single Page Application for two user roles (admin and distributor). It communicates with  exclusively over HTTP using Axios.

Key responsibilities:
- Admin dashboard: user management, package creation, route planning
- Distributor dashboard: daily route view, stop progression, status updates
- Token management: stores  in a cookie, relies on the backend's HttpOnly  cookie for silent refresh
- Map visualization via the HERE Maps API (frontend only)

— the documentation site

The  package is a Nextra-powered documentation site. It imports  as a workspace dependency to reuse UI primitives in documentation examples.

Backend internal layers

Every API endpoint under  follows a strict four-layer structure:

Layer responsibilities in detail

— the entry point

Rules:
- No SQL statements
- No business logic
- One try/catch block, always delegated to 
- Calls  before anything else on protected routes

— data transfer objects

Each DTO module exports a single  function. It:
- Checks field presence and types using guard functions from 
- Throws  on failure
- Returns a strongly typed DTO on success
- Never touches the database

— business logic

The service layer contains all decisions: existence checks, state machine transitions, orchestration of multiple repositories, email dispatch. It:
- Calls one or more repository functions
- Throws domain errors (, , etc.) when preconditions fail
- Never imports from  or constructs HTTP responses

— data access

The repository layer owns all SQL. It:
- Uses  prepared statements exclusively
- Returns domain types (never raw  to callers)
- Contains no business decisions or conditional logic beyond query construction

Shared infrastructure ()

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
|  | Domain error classes: , , , ,  |
|  | , , , , ,  +  |
|  | , , ,  |
|  | Type guards: , , , , , ,  |
|  | ,  (bcrypt wrapper) |
|  | Transactional email dispatch via Resend |
|  | ,  (Google Directions + Geocoding APIs) |
|  | State transition side effects: insert status log + send tracking email |

Authentication model

Refresh token rotation is implemented: every  call revokes the previous token and issues a new one. This prevents replay attacks if a refresh token is stolen.

Protected routes call  as the first statement. It:
1. Extracts the  header
2. Verifies the JWT signature and expiry
3. Checks the decoded role is in the allowed  array
4. Throws  (→ 401) or  (→ 403) otherwise

Email notifications

Emails are sent via Resend using custom HTML templates stored in . No external template engine. Templates include:

| Trigger | Template |
|---|---|
| New user created | Account activation link |
| Password reset requested | Reset link |
| Package assigned to route | Assignment notification to recipient |
| Package in transit | In-transit notification |
| Package delivered / undelivered / failed | Delivery outcome notification |

Route optimization

When an admin creates a route,  calls the Google Directions API with  to reorder stops for minimum travel time. The response waypoint order is mapped back to package stop positions, and estimated arrival times are calculated per stop and stored in the database.

> [!NOTE]
> Routes are capped at 20 stops. The system also carries over undelivered packages from previous days ("carryover" stops) when building a new route.

Inter-package communication

The three packages are share-nothing at runtime — they do not import each other's source code (except  importing  UI primitives). All runtime communication is:

-  → : HTTP (Axios, JSON)
-  → MySQL: TCP (mysql2 connection pool)
-  → Resend API: HTTPS
-  → Google APIs: HTTPS

> [!WARNING]
>  lists  as a workspace dependency. This creates a build-time coupling. Do not import  modules from  — the backend package uses  and other Node-only dependencies that will break the docs build.

Request Lifecycle

This page traces a single HTTP request end-to-end through the pakAG backend. The example used is  — one of the most complete flows, touching auth, validation, service logic, and the database.

Overview

Step 1 — HTTP in (Next.js edge)

Next.js receives the request and routes it to the exported  function in  based on the file-system path . No Express, no custom router.

The function signature is:

 is the standard Web API  object.  is a standard . The  helpers in  wrap  with pre-set status codes.

Step 2 — Auth guard ()

 (from ) executes synchronously:

1. Reads the  header.
2. Strips the  prefix to get the raw JWT string.
3. Calls  from the  library.
4. Decodes the payload and checks  is in the allowed roles array.

Throws on failure:

| Condition | Error thrown | HTTP status |
|---|---|---|
| Missing or malformed  header |  | 401 |
| JWT expired or invalid signature |  | 401 |
| Valid JWT but wrong role |  | 403 |

If  throws, execution jumps immediately to the  block — the body is never read.

Step 3 — Body parsing

Standard  call. If the body is not valid JSON,  throws a . The catch block will handle it via , which maps unknown errors to a 500 response.

> [!NOTE]
> Content-Type is not validated explicitly — if a client sends the wrong content type,  will still attempt to parse the raw body as JSON and throw  if it fails.

Step 4 — DTO validation ()

The DTO function (in ) receives  and:

1. Checks each required field exists and has the correct type using guards from :
   -  — must be a non-empty string
   -  — must match email regex
   -  — must be  or 
2. Returns a strongly typed  object.
3. Throws  if any check fails.

The DTO layer must never:
- Touch the database
- Import from 
- Be async (unless there is a documented exception)

Step 5 — Service layer ()

The service owns all business logic:

The service layer must never:
- Import  or construct HTTP responses
- Write raw SQL strings
- Return  — always map to domain types before returning

Step 6 — Repository layer (SQL)

Each repository function wraps one or more prepared SQL statements using the mysql2 connection pool from .

Rules enforced in this layer:
- All SQL uses  placeholders — never string interpolation
- Returns typed domain objects, not raw 
- No conditional logic, no side effects beyond the SQL operation

Step 7 — Response construction

 (from ) is:

The full response helper surface:

| Helper | Status | When to use |
|---|---|---|
|  | 200 | Successful GET or PATCH |
|  | 201 | Successful POST that creates a resource |
|  | 204 | Successful DELETE |
|  | 400 | DTO validation failure |
|  | 401 | Missing or invalid JWT |
|  | 403 | Valid JWT but wrong role |
|  | 404 | Resource does not exist |
|  | 409 | Duplicate / state conflict |
|  | 500 | Unexpected internal error |

Error flow

The  block in every  delegates to :

 inspects the error class and maps it to the appropriate HTTP status:

The complete error class hierarchy:

> [!WARNING]
> The 500 handler currently passes  to . This can leak internal details (MySQL table names, stack traces) to the client. Production deployments should always return  and log the detail server-side only — see the known bugs section in the docs.

Complete sequence diagram

Token refresh flow

When the  expires (15 minutes default), the frontend calls:

The backend:
1. Reads the  cookie
2. Looks it up in the  table (must be type , not revoked, not expired)
3. Issues a new  JWT
4. Rotates the refresh token (DELETE old, INSERT new in DB)
5. Sets the new  as an HttpOnly cookie
6. Returns the new  in the response body

> [!TIP]
> The frontend (React Query + Axios interceptors) handles token expiry transparently. When a 401 is received, it retries the refresh and then replays the original request.

en.title: Docs Audit
en.content:
Docs Audit

This page tracks the current state of the documentation site: what was verified, what is still unconfirmed, known risks, and architecture decisions for future maintainers.

Locale coverage status

The documentation navigation is translated across all supported locales, but the content depth is not yet synchronized. English is the source of truth for the deeper page set.

| Locale | Current coverage | Recommended next action |
| --- | --- | --- |
| EN | Complete/deeper coverage: 31 MDX pages, including detailed child pages for API, backend, frontend, and architecture topics | Keep EN as the verified source while parity work continues |
| ES | Partial/top-level coverage: 17 MDX pages, mostly section landing pages | Translate or summarize the missing EN child pages, or mark each pending translation explicitly |
| EUS | Partial/top-level coverage: 17 MDX pages, mostly section landing pages | Translate or summarize the missing EN child pages, or mark each pending translation explicitly |

Locale parity checklist

- When adding a docs page, create or update the corresponding , , and  content in the same change.
- If a full translation is not ready, add the locale page with a translated heading and an explicit  /  /  note.
- Keep navigation,  entries, and page links aligned across all three locales.

What was verified

The following claims were cross-referenced against the actual codebase under , , and :

- Monorepo structure: three packages (, , ) under pnpm workspaces. Verified from .
- Backend endpoint inventory: all endpoints in the API reference were verified against  files.
- Auth flows: access token (Bearer JWT, 15 min) + refresh token (HTTP-only cookie, 7 days) confirmed from  and .
- Package state machine:  confirmed from .
- Email side effects on status change: confirmed from .
- Token types: , , ,  confirmed from .
- Database name inconsistency:  creates database as ;  documents it as . Marked as Needs verification in environment docs.
- Google Directions origin: coordinates hardcoded in  — confirmed risk documented in security and environment pages.
- Frontend API client file paths: all  paths are referenced as documented; exact TypeScript type locations are verified from .

What is still unverified (Needs verification)

The following items could not be confirmed without access to a running environment or full integration test:

- Exact request/response payload schemas for admin-only endpoints that the current frontend does not call (, , , etc.).
- Pagination defaults for list endpoints (, , ).
- Error response variants beyond  — full error shape per status code.
- Production domain and CORS configuration (documented as risk, not confirmed).
- Activation URL: docs note it reuses ; exact behavior in a live environment is unverified.
- Frontend admin-facing UI screens: some screen locations referenced in domain/frontend pages could not be pinned to specific file paths.

Known risks

| Severity | Risk | Reference |
| --- | --- | --- |
| High |  has a hardcoded fallback in  — if not set in production, the fallback is publicly visible in the repo | Security page, CLAUDE.md §4.2 |
| High |  has the same hardcoded fallback problem | Security page, CLAUDE.md §4.3 |
| Medium |  forwards internal error messages to HTTP responses in 500 cases | CLAUDE.md §4.5 |
| Medium | Rate limiting packages installed () but not implemented | CLAUDE.md §7.2 |
| Low | MySQL env vars are not validated at startup — silent failures possible | CLAUDE.md §4.4 |
| Low |  uses TypeScript  assertion without a startup guard | CLAUDE.md §4.10 |

Docs site architecture decisions

- Custom layout — the docs do not use  layout. Instead, a fully custom sidebar, header, and content layout is implemented in . This was chosen to allow full design control.
- Locale routing — locale is the first URL segment (, , ). Middleware at  redirects locale-less paths to .
- Search — implemented as a static client-side page-title search via . No full-text index. Ctrl+K / Cmd+K opens the search modal.
- Table of contents —  reads DOM headings after mount (scoped to ). Headings get stable IDs from the  function in .
- Mobile TOC —  renders a button below the header that opens a bottom drawer with the TOC and a shortcut to search.
- i18n — dictionaries live in . Adding a new nav item requires updating  interface and all three locale files.
- MDX heading IDs — , ,  MDX components are overridden in  to auto-generate  attributes via .

Build and search status

| Feature | Status |
| --- | --- |
| Root  redirect to  | Working via  + middleware |
| Locale-prefixed routing | Working — , ,  |
| Locale fallback (e.g.  → ) | Working via  |
| Per-page TOC (desktop) | Working — right-side sticky via  |
| Per-page TOC (mobile) | Working — bottom drawer via  |
| Ctrl+K search | Working — static title search via  |
| Heading anchor links | Working — IDs generated via  in  |
| Code block copy button | Working — via  |
| Mobile sidebar | Working — via  |
| View transitions | Working — CSS view transitions on page navigation |

How future agents should update docs

1. Adding a new backend endpoint: update . Add request/response examples. Verify from the actual  and  files. Mark anything unverified with .
2. Adding a new frontend page or hook: update .
3. Adding a new doc section or page: create/update the content in all three locale trees (, , and ) in the same change. If full translation is not complete, add a translated heading and mark the page as .
4. Keeping locale depth aligned: when EN gets a deeper child page, add matching ES/EUS placeholders or translations so the locale page counts and nav structure do not drift silently.
5. Fixing a broken link: all internal links should be locale-prefixed (e.g. ). Locale-less links also work via middleware redirect, but explicit locale links are preferred.
6. Keeping docs truthful: never document behavior that cannot be verified from the codebase. Use  callouts with "Needs verification" for anything uncertain.

en.title: Backend — Auth Flow
en.content:
Backend — Auth Flow

pakAG uses a dual-token JWT strategy: a short-lived access token carried as a Bearer header, and a long-lived refresh token stored in an HttpOnly cookie and persisted in the database.

Token overview

| Token | Where stored | Lifetime | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access token | Client memory (Authorization header) | 15 minutes (default) | Authenticate every API request |
| Refresh token | HttpOnly cookie +  DB table | 7 days (default) | Issue new access tokens without re-login |

The access token is a signed JWT. The refresh token is a random UUID — it is not a JWT; it is an opaque string that is looked up in the database on each refresh request.

---

Full flow diagram

---

Step 1 — Login

Route

The  is extracted from the service response and set as a cookie — it never appears in the JSON body.

Service

> [!NOTE]
> The  is used to prevent timing-based user enumeration: if the user does not exist, bcrypt would normally be skipped (fast), making it easy to detect which emails are registered by measuring response time. Running bcrypt against the dummy hash equalizes timing for both cases.

Repository

---

Step 2 — Authenticated requests

Every protected endpoint calls  before doing anything else:

The access token payload is:

Verification is purely local — no database lookup. The JWT signature is verified against . If the token has expired,  throws and  rethrows as  (→ HTTP 401).

Usage in a route handler

---

Step 3 — Token refresh

When the access token expires (HTTP 401 from any endpoint), the client silently calls the refresh endpoint:

Refresh service — token rotation

Token rotation means every refresh call issues a brand-new refresh token and revokes the previous one. If an attacker somehow obtains a refresh token and uses it, the legitimate user's next refresh attempt will fail (the token is revoked), making the theft detectable.

---

Step 4 — Logout

Logout revokes the refresh token in the database and instructs the client to delete the cookie:

The third argument to  is  — it appends   headers that instruct the browser to delete the named cookie.

After logout, any attempt to use the revoked refresh token returns .

---

The  table

All four token types share a single table. This is the actual schema from :

|  |  |  | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
|  | ✓ | — | Session token for POST /api/auth/refresh |
|  | — | ✓ | Opaque token for public package tracking |

Backend — Overview

The pakAG backend is a Next.js 15 App Router application that exclusively uses Route Handlers as its API layer. There are no pages, no React server components serving HTML — only JSON endpoints.

Tech stack

| TypeScript | Type safety end-to-end |

| Resend | Transactional email (activation, reset, status notifications) |
| Google Directions API | Route optimization and stop ordering |
| Google Geocoding API | Address → lat/lng conversion for package creation |

Monorepo location

Folder structure per endpoint

Every use-case lives in its own folder under :

en.title: Backend — Layered Architecture Patterns
en.content:
Backend — Layered Architecture Patterns

Every endpoint in pakAG follows the same four-layer structure. This page walks through the pattern using the  endpoint as a concrete reference — it is the canonical example used throughout the codebase.

Why a layered pattern?

A flat handler that does validation, queries the database, applies business rules, and sends emails is impossible to reason about and impossible to test in isolation. The four layers enforce a single concern per file:

| Layer | Concern | What it must NOT do |
|---|---|---|
|  | HTTP boundary | SQL, business logic, validation logic |
|  | Input validation | SQL, side effects, business rules |
|  | Business logic | SQL, HTTP concerns |
|  | SQL access | Business decisions, HTTP concerns |

This makes it possible to read any file and immediately understand its scope.

Folder layout

The folder for DTOs is always named  (plural). The export function is always named .

> [!NOTE]
> Some older endpoints use  (singular). The correct convention is  (plural), matching .

---

Layer 1 — 

Define the data shapes first.  holds the domain interfaces for this endpoint only — not global types (those live in ).

The DTO interface describes exactly the validated, trusted data that flows from  to . The response interface describes what the HTTP layer returns to the client.

---

Layer 2 —  — Input validation

The DTO module answers one question: is the request body structurally valid?

Why no Zod?

Zod (and similar schema libraries) add significant bundle weight and a runtime dependency for a validation task that pure TypeScript guards handle cleanly. The project uses a set of hand-written type guards in :

These guards are composable, tree-shakeable, and produce zero runtime overhead beyond the checks themselves.

Rules for DTOs

- Function signature is always 
- Body parameter is  — never 
- Throw  (from ) for every constraint that fails
- Never call the database from a DTO — existence checks belong in 
- Return the typed, narrowed DTO object on success

> [!WARNING]
> Some endpoints in the codebase violate this rule by calling  or  from DTOs — these functions execute SQL queries. This is a known architectural violation tracked in the project's bug list.

---

Layer 3 —  — SQL access

The repository is the only place SQL is written. It receives typed inputs, executes prepared statements, and returns typed row data.

Why prepared statements?

 (not ) uses server-side prepared statements, which:

1. Prevent SQL injection — the database driver separates query structure from user data
2. Can be cached by MySQL — repeated executions skip the parse step
3. Type-safe parameters — mysql2 maps TypeScript types to MySQL column types

The repository uses  only for  statements where the parameters are known-safe and the query shape is fixed.

Rules for repositories

- No  statements, no business logic, no error throwing for business reasons
- Accept typed inputs, return typed outputs using  extensions
- Map column names to TypeScript properties in the query with  aliases where needed (e.g. )
- Never import from  — the only errors that should escape are unexpected DB errors

---

Layer 4 —  — Business logic

The service owns all business decisions: existence checks, conflict detection, state transitions, orchestrating side effects.

What belongs in a service

- Existence checks — "does this email already exist?"
- State transition rules — "is this status change allowed from the current state?"
- Derived data — hashing passwords, generating tokens, computing ETAs
- Side effects — sending emails, writing to audit logs, updating related records
- Cross-repository coordination — calling multiple repos within a single transaction scope

What does NOT belong in a service

- SQL strings — always delegate to a repository function
- HTTP concerns — no  or  objects
- Input parsing — trust that the DTO is already validated

---

Layer 5 —  — HTTP boundary

The route handler is the orchestrator. It follows a strict, predictable sequence:

The pattern is always: requireAuth → validateDto → service → res.xxx → catch handleError.

Pass  to restrict to admins,  for distributors, or both to allow either. Omit  to allow any authenticated user.

Response helpers ()

mapping

> [!WARNING]
> In the current implementation, unexpected 500 errors include the raw error message in the response. This can expose internal details (table names, stack traces) to clients. In production, always return a generic  message and log internally only.

---

Error flow end-to-end

Here is what happens when a client sends  with a malformed body:

If the email was valid but already exists:

---

Error classes reference

Always import from . Never throw  for a condition that is a known business failure — that turns it into an unclassified 500.

---

Checklist for a new endpoint

When creating :

- [ ] Create  — define  and 
- [ ] Create  — export 
- [ ] Create  — SQL only, no logic
- [ ] Create  — logic only, no SQL
- [ ] Create  — 
- [ ] Run  — must pass with zero errors
- [ ] No  — explicit or implicit

en.content:
Deployment

This page covers building each package for production, the environment variables required, hosting options, and common deployment pitfalls.

---

Production build commands

Run these from the repo root. Each command triggers  inside the respective package.

After building, start each service with its  script (Next.js production server):

> [!TIP]
> In a process manager like PM2, map each package to a  command and assign ports via  environment variable (e.g. ).

---

Port assignments

| Service | Development port | Recommended production port |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | 3001 | 3001 (behind reverse proxy) |
|  | 3000 | 3000 (behind reverse proxy) or served via CDN |
|  | 3002 | 3002 (behind reverse proxy) |

Use a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Traefik) in front of all three services to terminate TLS and route by hostname or path prefix.

---

Required environment variables in production

backend-js

All of these must be set via your deployment platform's secrets manager (never committed to the repo).

| Variable | Required | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | ✅ | Production DB host or socket |

|  | optional | Default  |

|  | ✅ | Required for all email flows |
|  | ✅ | Required for route creation and geocoding |

> [!WARNING]
>  and  have hardcoded fallbacks in . These fallbacks are visible in the public repository. If you deploy without overriding them, any attacker can forge JWT tokens and know the initial password of every created user.

frontend-app

| Variable | Required | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | ✅ in dev | Production code falls back to  when . Override to change the API domain. |
|  | ✅ | HERE Maps key for map rendering |
|  | optional | Link to the docs site |

---

MySQL in production

Connection pooling

The backend uses  with a connection pool (). The pool is a module-level singleton — it is created once when the module first loads and reused across all requests.

Key considerations:

- Set  to match your MySQL server's  capacity. A typical starting value is .
- In serverless environments (Vercel, AWS Lambda), each function invocation may spawn a new pool. This can exhaust MySQL connections quickly. Consider using PlanetScale or Neon with their HTTP drivers, or a dedicated connection proxy like ProxySQL or RDS Proxy.
- In self-hosted deployments (a single long-running Node process), the pool behaves correctly as-is.

SSL / TLS

If your production MySQL instance requires TLS (common on managed services like AWS RDS, GCP Cloud SQL, Azure Database for MySQL), add SSL configuration to the pool options in :

And add  (PEM content) to your secrets.

---

Next.js hosting options

Option A — Vercel (recommended for frontend-app)

The frontend is a standard Next.js App Router application and deploys to Vercel with zero configuration.

1. Connect the repository in the Vercel dashboard.
2. Set Root Directory to .
3. Add all  environment variables in the Vercel project settings.
4. Deploy.

> [!NOTE]
> Vercel does not run the backend. Deploy  separately on a VPS or container platform so it has a persistent MySQL connection.

Option B — Self-hosted (VPS / Docker)

Run each service as a long-lived process behind nginx:

Use Certbot to provision TLS certificates automatically.

---

Cookie and CORS configuration

The authentication flow uses an HttpOnly  cookie issued by the backend and sent automatically by the browser on every  call.

For this to work cross-origin in production:

| Setting | Development | Production |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  (refresh cookie) |  |  |
|  (refresh cookie) |  |  (requires HTTPS) |
| CORS  |  |  |
| CORS  |  | frontend origin (e.g. ) |

If the frontend and backend are on different subdomains, the browser will only send the  cookie if TLS is active end-to-end. An HTTP-only deployment will silently drop the refresh cookie after login.

> [!WARNING]
> Never set  when  is required. The browser will reject such responses. Always specify the exact frontend origin.

---

Health check endpoints

There is no dedicated  endpoint in the current codebase. The simplest proxy health check is:

This endpoint always responds with a JSON body (either  or the authenticated user). A non- response confirms the backend is up and MySQL is reachable.

---

Common deployment pitfalls

| Symptom | Cause | Fix |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Refresh token loop in production |  cookie not sent over HTTP | Enable TLS; ensure  flag is set |
| CORS error on credentialed request |  with  | Set exact frontend origin in CORS config |
|  on backend start | MySQL not reachable from deployment host | Check , firewall rules, VPC peering |
| Route creation always fails |  not set or quota exceeded | Verify key in GCP console; check billing |
| Emails not sent |  missing or domain not verified | Add key to secrets; verify sending domain in Resend dashboard |
| Token forgery risk |  using source-code fallback | Set a strong unique  in your secrets manager |

How Next.js loads environment variables

1.  — base defaults, can be committed

> [!NOTE]

en.content:
Auth Flow

This page documents how the pakAG frontend authenticates users, stores the access token,
attaches it to every request, silently refreshes it before expiry, and cleans up on logout.

---

Overview

---

Token Storage

Access token — JS-accessible cookie

The access token is stored in a first-party cookie (not , not memory).

The  constant is  in production and  in development
(from ).

| Attribute | Dev | Prod |
|---|---|---|
|  |  |  |
|  | ✗ | ✓ |
|  | ✗ | ✗ |
|  | 900 s | 900 s |
|  |  |  |

> [!NOTE]
> The access token cookie is not  — the frontend reads it via 
> to attach it to every Axios request. This is intentional; the attack surface is mitigated by
>  (no cross-site request forgery) and the short 15-minute TTL.

Refresh token — backend HttpOnly cookie

The refresh token is managed entirely by the backend:

- Set as  (dev) or  (prod).
- Never readable by JavaScript.
- Automatically sent by the browser when  on the Axios instance.
- Rotated on every  call (old token is revoked in the database).

---

Axios Instance Configuration

A dedicated  (identical config, no interceptors) is used only for the refresh
call to avoid interceptor loops.

---

Request Interceptor — Attaching the Bearer Header

The interceptor reads the access token from the cookie on every request and injects
 into the request headers.

Skipped endpoints

Some endpoints must never receive (or trigger refresh for) the access token:

These paths are defined as constants in :

| Constant | Value |
|---|---|
|  |  |
|  |  |
|  |  |
|  |  |

---

Response Interceptor — Refresh on 401

When any non-skipped request returns , the interceptor silently refreshes the access
token and replays the original request once:

Concurrent request deduplication

If multiple requests fail with  at the same time, the refresh is only called once.
A shared  variable ensures all waiting requests reuse the same pending
refresh:

---

Background Token Refresh

 is mounted in the  layout and runs a React Query polling loop
that proactively refreshes the token before it expires:

This means the access token is refreshed proactively every 12 minutes. The response
interceptor acts as a safety net only if the token expires unexpectedly (e.g. after a
long background-tab period).

---

Login Flow

After a successful login:
1. Access token is stored in the cookie.
2. All  queries are invalidated.
3.  is prefetched so the  resolves immediately.

---

Logout Flow

 (not ) is used so the local state is always cleaned up — even if the
 request fails. After the cookie and cache are wiped, the 
will find no user and redirect to .

> [!NOTE]
> The backend revokes the refresh token in the database on . Even if a
> stale refresh token cookie somehow remains, the backend will reject it on the next
>  call.

Redirect After Login

> and consuming this redirect intent was not found in the source files reviewed. Verify

en.content:
State Management — React Query

pakAG uses TanStack React Query v5 as the single source of truth for all server state.
There is no Redux, Zustand, or React Context for remote data — everything goes through the
query cache.

---

QueryClient Setup

The client is created in  and mounted once at the root
via .

Key defaults

| Setting | Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
|  | max 1, skip for 4xx | Avoid hammering the API on client errors |
|  |  | Data stays fresh when user returns to tab |
|  |  | Recover after network drop |
|  | 10 minutes | Keep cache warm during navigation |
|  |  | Mutations are side-effectful — never auto-replay |

> [!NOTE]
> The  is created inside  (not ).
> Passing the factory function prevents a new client being created on every render during SSR.

---

Query Key Convention

All query keys live in  — one file per domain. Keys are expressed as
factory objects returning  tuples. This makes  scoped and safe.

Key hierarchy

> [!TIP]
> When a mutation affects multiple domains (e.g. updating a package status also invalidates
> the route), call  for each root key. See the mutation example
> below.

---

Query Options Pattern

Query options objects are defined in  using  from React
Query. This gives TypeScript inference for  and  without duplication.

Hooks import these options objects rather than duplicating query keys and fetch functions:

---

Mutation Pattern

Mutations follow a consistent structure:

1.  calls the API module function.
2.  invalidates the affected query keys via .
3. The hook returns the full  result (callers access , ,
   , etc.).

Login mutation — cache warm-up on success

The  hook also prefetches the  query immediately after a successful login so
the  resolves without an extra network round-trip:

Logout mutation — full cache wipe

 uses  (not ) so the cache is wiped even if the backend
request fails:

---

Axios API Client

All API calls go through a single Axios instance defined in
:

A separate  (no interceptors, same ) is used exclusively for the
 call to avoid infinite retry loops.

---

Error Handling

shape

Every error thrown by the API layer is normalised into an :

The  utility in 
converts any thrown value (Axios error,  instance, unknown) into this shape before
it reaches React Query.

Surfacing errors in components

Retry behaviour

The  suppresses retries for . These are
deterministic client-side errors — retrying would not change the outcome.
Network errors () get one automatic retry.

> [!WARNING]
> There is no global error boundary wired to React Query's  at the 
> level. Each component is responsible for reading and displaying . Consider adding a
>  error observer if you need app-wide toast notifications.

en.content:
Monorepo Structure

pakAG is a pnpm workspace monorepo with three packages. The root directory does not contain application code — it only holds workspace configuration, the database schema, and root-level scripts.

Root layout

Workspace declaration

 declares workspace membership:

All three packages are in the root directory (not nested under ). pnpm treats each directory listed here as a workspace package and hoists their dependencies to  at the root where possible.

Root 

The root  has no runtime dependencies. All dependencies live in the individual packages. The  flag prevents accidental publishing.

Root scripts

| Script | Command | What it does |
|---|---|---|
|  |  | Starts the API in dev mode on port 3001 |
|  |  | Starts the UI in dev mode on port 3000 |
|  |  | Starts the docs site in dev mode on port 3002 |

 runs the named script in the matching workspace package. The package names are defined in each  under the  field.

> [!TIP]
> To start all three packages simultaneously, open three terminal tabs and run each script, or use a process manager like  with three panes. There is no root  script — this is intentional: starting only what you need keeps resource usage low.

en.title: Project Overview
en.content:
Project Overview

What pakAG is

pakAG is a delivery operations platform for small-to-medium logistics operations. It centralizes the full lifecycle of a package — from creation to doorstep delivery — for an organization that manages its own fleet of distributors.

The system is composed of three parts:

1. An internal web application () used by admins and distributors to manage packages, plan routes, and report delivery outcomes.
2. A REST API backend () that enforces all business rules, persists data, and dispatches email notifications.
3. A public tracking endpoint that allows package recipients to check their delivery status without logging in.

> [!NOTE]
> The project was developed as part of a competition ("erronka") — hence the repository name . The product name in business logic and documentation is pakAG.

Problem it solves

Before pakAG, logistics operations managed package assignments, delivery sequences, and status updates through spreadsheets or disconnected tools. pakAG replaces that with:

- Centralized package records — every package has a lifecycle: 
- Route optimization — admins assign packages to a distributor for a given day, and the system calls the Google Directions API to compute the optimal stop order
- Real-time status visibility — every state transition is logged, and recipients receive automatic email notifications at key milestones
- Public tracking — recipients get a unique tracking URL when their package is created; no account required

Business domain

Entities

| Entity | Description |
|---|---|
| User | System user with role  or ;  flag controls login access |
| Package | A physical parcel with a recipient, an address, and a current status |
| Address | Geocoded delivery address — stored with / for route optimization |
| Route | A day's delivery plan for one distributor — contains an ordered list of stops |
| Stop | A single delivery stop on a route — links a route to a package, holds order and estimated arrival |
| PackageStatusLog | Immutable audit trail — every status change records timestamp, new status, and optionally a note |
| Token | Opaque hex tokens for: , , ,  |

Package status machine

Each transition triggers a side effect: an entry in  and an email to the recipient.

Route lifecycle

1. Admin creates a route: selects a distributor user and a date, picks the packages to include (max 20 stops).
2. The backend fetches any carryover packages — packages assigned to that distributor from previous days still in  status — and prepends them to the stop list.
3. The Google Directions API is called with  to compute the optimal waypoint order.
4. Estimated arrival times are calculated per stop and stored in the database.
5. The distributor sees their daily route in the frontend, starts it, arrives at each stop, and marks packages as delivered / undelivered / failed.
6. At end of day, any undelivered stops are eligible to carry over to the next route.

Users and roles

Admin

Admins manage the full operational scope. They:

- Create and manage user accounts (admins and distributors)
- Create, update, and delete packages
- Assign packages to routes
- View all package and route history
- Access system-wide logs and daily delivery summaries

Admins access the internal dashboard in .

Distributor

Distributors execute the daily delivery work. They:

- View their assigned route for the day
- Progress through stops in the optimized order
- Mark packages as delivered, undelivered, or failed at each stop
- Record actual arrival time at each stop
- Can continue a previous day's pending route if undelivered stops were carried over

Distributors access the same internal app as admins but see a different, role-scoped view.

Recipient (package recipient)

Recipients are not system users — they have no account and no login. They interact with pakAG only through:

- Email notifications at each status transition (assigned, intransit, delivered/failed)
- The public tracking page** — a URL containing a one-time tracking token that shows the current package status and history

The tracking token is created when the package is created and expires after a configurable number of days (default: 30).

Key workflows

Creating a package (admin)

1. Admin submits: recipient name, email, delivery address, package description
2. Backend geocodes the address via Google Geocoding API → stores /
3. Backend generates a tracking token and stores it in the  table
4. Backend sends a "package created" notification email to the recipient with the tracking URL
5. Package is in  status until assigned to a route

Activating a new user account

1. Admin creates a user via 
2. Backend stores the user with  and a hashed default password
3. Backend generates an  and sends an activation email
4. User clicks the link and POSTs to  with their chosen password
5. Backend verifies the token, hashes the password, sets , revokes the token

Password reset

1. User visits "forgot password" and submits their email
2. Backend responds with a generic message (does not reveal whether the email exists)
3. If the email matches a user, a  is generated and an email is sent
4. User clicks the link and POSTs to  with a new password
5. Backend verifies, changes the password, revokes the token

Technical stack summary

| Layer | Technology | Version |
|---|---|---|
| API runtime | Next.js Route Handlers | 16.2.4 |
| Language | TypeScript | 5.x |
| Database | MySQL | 8.x |
| DB client | mysql2 | 3.22.0 |
| Auth | JWT (HS256) + bcrypt | jsonwebtoken 9.0.2 / bcrypt 6.0.0 |
| Email | Resend | 6.12.2 |
| Maps | Google Directions + Geocoding API | — |
| Frontend framework | Next.js App Router | 16.2.4 |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS | 4.x |
| Data fetching (frontend) | TanStack React Query + Axios | 5.x / 1.x |
| Monorepo | pnpm workspaces | 10.20.0 |
| Documentation | Nextra | 4.6.1 |

What pakAG is NOT

- Not a public SaaS product — it is designed for a single organization with internal users
- Not a mobile app — the frontend is a web application (though responsive)
- Not a warehouse management system — it handles packages after they leave the warehouse
- Not a real-time tracking system — status updates are manual, entered by the distributor

> [!WARNING]
> Some UI labels in  are currently in Basque (Euskara). This is intentional for the target region. This documentation site is in English for developer onboarding consistency.

en.title: Troubleshooting
en.content:
Troubleshooting

Backend won't start

Symptom:  or similar on .

Cause: Path aliases not resolved or  paths not matching.

Fix: Verify  at  has  under . If missing, check that  extends the correct base.

---

Missing or undefined environment variables

Symptom:  values in logs, DB connect errors, email send errors, JWT errors like .

Fix:
1. Copy  to  in .
2. Fill in all values — do not leave anything as-is from the example.
3. Restart the dev server after changes.

Critical vars that have hardcoded fallbacks (unsafe in prod, but will silently work in dev):
-  — uses known fallback if unset
-  — uses known fallback if unset

Vars with no fallback that will crash at runtime:
-  — email send will fail
-  — geocoding and route optimization will fail
- MySQL vars — DB pool will fail at first query

---

Database connection errors

Symptom: , , or  at runtime.

Checklist:
1. MySQL server is running on .
2.  /  credentials are correct.
3. The database exists.  creates it as  — run  if needed.
4.  env var matches the actual database name ( vs  — they differ between  and ).
5. SSL setting:  sets . If your MySQL instance requires a specific cert, adjust this.

---

TypeScript check fails ( errors)

Symptom: Type errors when running  in .

Common causes and fixes:

| Error | Fix |
| --- | --- |
|  | Check that the DTO interface and the types file are in sync |
|  | No  allowed — add an explicit interface |
|  | Check  paths |
| Type error in  | Verify  return type is used correctly |

---

401 on every request after login

Symptom: Login succeeds (returns ), but subsequent API calls return 401.

Checklist:
1. The frontend is storing the access token correctly in the  cookie.
2. The Axios request interceptor is reading from the cookie and setting .
3. The token isn't expired — access token lifetime is 15 minutes.
4. The backend is on a different origin — check CORS config and .

---

Refresh token not working / user repeatedly kicked to login

Symptom: User is logged out after 15 minutes despite  being active.

Checklist:
1. Open DevTools → Application → Cookies. Confirm  cookie exists with  flag.
2. Confirm  is not blocked by CORS. In dev, backend must allow the frontend origin with .
3. Confirm the refresh token row is not revoked/expired in the  table.
4. Check clock skew — if server time and client time differ by more than the token lifetime, tokens may appear expired immediately.
5. Confirm  is set in the Axios client.

---

CORS errors

Symptom:  header missing, preflight fails.

Fix: In  (or equivalent CORS middleware), ensure:
- The frontend origin is allowed explicitly (not  when using credentials).
-  is set.
- Preflight  requests return 200.

In production, both apps must be on HTTPS for  cookies to work.

---

Email not sent after package creation / status change

Symptom: No email arrives, but the operation succeeds.

Checklist:
1.  is set and valid.
2. The sender domain () is verified in Resend.
3. Check Resend dashboard for send failures or bounces.
4. Status change emails are conditional — only specific transitions trigger emails. See Domain Model for the email trigger table.

---

Route creation fails (Google Maps error)

Symptom:  returns 500 or an error related to Directions API.

Checklist:
1.  is set and the key has Directions API and Geocoding API enabled in Google Cloud Console.
2. Billing is active on the Google Cloud project.
3. The API key has no IP/referer restriction that blocks the backend server IP.
4. Package addresses were geocoded successfully on creation — if / are 0 or NULL, geocoding failed at package creation time.

---

Geocoding returns wrong location

Symptom: Package appears at wrong coordinates on the map.

Cause: Google Geocoding matched a different address, or the address is ambiguous.

Fix: Check the package's  row in the DB. Correct / manually if needed via a direct DB update (no endpoint currently exposes raw coordinate editing).

---

Route optimization exceeds max stops

Symptom: Route creation is rejected even with fewer packages than expected.

Cause: The route service adds carryover packages (assigned packages from previous days for the same distributor) to the stop list automatically. Total stops are capped at 20.

Fix: Reduce  in the request to leave room for carryover, or clear the distributor's backlog first.

---

Package status log missing after update

Symptom:  returns no logs after  call.

Cause: Log insertion happens in . If it throws and is caught silently, the status update may succeed but no log is written.

Fix: Check backend console for errors from . Verify DB constraints allow the log insert (e.g.,  user exists).

---

Frontend build fails

Symptom:  errors with TypeScript or module errors.

Fix:


---

Docs site search not finding a page

Symptom: Ctrl+K search returns no results for a known page.

Cause: Search is static and based on  titles in . If you add a new page without adding it to , it won't appear in search.

Fix: Add the new page to  in  and update all three i18n dictionaries. See Docs Audit for the full procedure.

---

pnpm version conflicts

Symptom:  or workspace script not found.

Fix:


All workspace package commands must be run from the monorepo root using  flags or the root-level scripts in .

es.title: Flujo de trabajo de documentación con IA
es.content:
Flujo de trabajo de documentación con IA

Roles y modelo de colaboración

Codex (constructor principal de documentación)

- Escanea el repositorio y verifica afirmaciones técnicas contra los archivos fuente.
- Construye y mantiene la arquitectura de documentación en .
- Produce páginas técnicas completas y estructuradas.

Copilot (traducción y secciones pequeñas)

- Traduce el inglés fuente al español y al euskara.
- Gestiona expansiones pequeñas y refinamientos de formato.
- No debe modificar afirmaciones técnicas básicas sin verificación.

Claude (revisión y control de calidad editorial)

- Revisa la corrección, coherencia y legibilidad.
- Señala lagunas, contradicciones y referencias faltantes.
- Recomienda mejoras estructurales para la mantenibilidad.

Reglas no negociables

- Ningún agente IA debe inventar comportamiento del sistema.
- Todas las áreas inciertas deben marcarse explícitamente como Requiere verificación.
- Cada afirmación técnica debe ser rastreable al código actual del repositorio.

Secuencia de traspaso sugerida

1. Codex escribe/actualiza las páginas fuente en inglés.
2. Claude revisa y solicita correcciones.
3. Copilot traduce los diccionarios/contenido una vez que la fuente es estable.
4. Codex realiza el pase final de consistencia y publica.

es.title: Auditoria de docs
es.content:
Auditoria de docs

Esta pagina registra el estado actual del sitio de documentacion: que se verifico, que sigue sin confirmarse, riesgos conocidos y decisiones de arquitectura para futuros mantenedores.

Estado de cobertura por locale

La navegacion de la documentacion esta traducida en todos los locales soportados, pero la profundidad del contenido todavia no esta sincronizada. Ingles es la fuente de verdad para el conjunto de paginas mas profundo.

| Locale | Cobertura actual | Siguiente accion recomendada |
| --- | --- | --- |
| EN | Cobertura completa/mas profunda: 31 paginas MDX, incluyendo paginas hijas detalladas para API, backend, frontend y arquitectura | Mantener EN como fuente verificada mientras avanza el trabajo de paridad |
| ES | Cobertura parcial/top-level: 17 paginas MDX, principalmente paginas landing de seccion | Traducir o resumir las paginas hijas faltantes de EN, o marcar cada traduccion pendiente explicitamente |
| EUS | Cobertura parcial/top-level: 17 paginas MDX, principalmente paginas landing de seccion | Traducir o resumir las paginas hijas faltantes de EN, o marcar cada traduccion pendiente explicitamente |

Checklist de paridad de locales

- Al anadir una pagina de docs, crear o actualizar el contenido correspondiente en ,  y  dentro del mismo cambio.
- Si la traduccion completa no esta lista, anadir la pagina del locale con un heading traducido y una nota explicita  /  / .
- Mantener alineados la navegacion, las entradas  y los links de pagina en los tres locales.

Que se verifico

Las siguientes afirmaciones se cruzaron contra el codigo real bajo ,  y :

- Estructura monorepo: tres paquetes (, , ) bajo pnpm workspaces. Verificado desde .
- Inventario de endpoints backend: todos los endpoints de la referencia API se verificaron contra archivos .
- Flujos de auth: access token (Bearer JWT, 15 min) + refresh token (cookie HTTP-only, 7 dias) confirmados desde  y .
- Maquina de estados de paquetes:  confirmada desde .
- Efectos secundarios de email al cambiar estado: confirmados desde .
- Tipos de token: , , ,  confirmados desde .
- Inconsistencia del nombre de BD:  crea la base como ;  la documenta como . Marcado como Needs verification en docs de environment.
- Origen de Google Directions: coordenadas hardcodeadas en ; riesgo confirmado y documentado en security y environment.
- Rutas de archivos del cliente API frontend: todas las rutas  se referencian como estan documentadas; las ubicaciones exactas de tipos TypeScript se verificaron desde .

Que sigue sin verificar (Needs verification)

Los siguientes items no pudieron confirmarse sin acceso a un entorno en ejecucion o test de integracion completo:

- Schemas exactos de request/response para endpoints solo-admin que el frontend actual no llama (, , , etc.).
- Defaults de paginacion para endpoints de listado (, , ).
- Variantes de respuesta de error mas alla de : forma completa de error por status code.
- Dominio de produccion y configuracion CORS (documentado como riesgo, no confirmado).
- URL de activacion: las docs indican que reutiliza ; el comportamiento exacto en un entorno vivo no esta verificado.
- Pantallas UI de admin en frontend: algunas ubicaciones de pantallas mencionadas en domain/frontend no pudieron fijarse a paths concretos.

Riesgos conocidos

| Severidad | Riesgo | Referencia |
| --- | --- | --- |
| High |  tiene un fallback hardcodeado en ; si no se define en produccion, el fallback es visible publicamente en el repo | Security page, CLAUDE.md §4.2 |
| High |  tiene el mismo problema de fallback hardcodeado | Security page, CLAUDE.md §4.3 |
| Medium |  reenvia mensajes internos de error en respuestas HTTP 500 | CLAUDE.md §4.5 |
| Medium | Paquetes de rate limiting instalados () pero no implementados | CLAUDE.md §7.2 |
| Low | Las variables env de MySQL no se validan al arrancar; posibles fallos silenciosos | CLAUDE.md §4.4 |
| Low |  usa asercion TypeScript  sin guard al arrancar | CLAUDE.md §4.10 |

Decisiones de arquitectura del sitio de docs

- Layout custom: las docs no usan el layout de . En su lugar,  implementa sidebar, header y layout de contenido totalmente custom para permitir control total de diseno.
- Rutas por locale: el locale es el primer segmento de URL (, , ). El middleware en  redirige paths sin locale a .
- Busqueda: implementada como busqueda estatica client-side por titulo de pagina mediante . No hay indice full-text. Ctrl+K / Cmd+K abre el modal.
- Tabla de contenidos:  lee headings del DOM tras mount (limitado a ). Los headings reciben IDs estables mediante  en .
- TOC movil:  renderiza un boton bajo el header que abre un drawer inferior con el TOC y un acceso a busqueda.
- i18n: los diccionarios viven en . Anadir un item de nav requiere actualizar la interfaz  y los tres archivos de locale.
- IDs de headings MDX: los componentes MDX , ,  se sobrescriben en  para autogenerar atributos  mediante .

Estado de build y busqueda

| Feature | Estado |
| --- | --- |
| Redirect raiz  a  | Funciona via  + middleware |
| Rutas con prefijo locale | Funcionan: , ,  |
| Fallback de locale (p. ej.  -> ) | Funciona via  |
| TOC por pagina (desktop) | Funciona: sticky lateral derecho via  |
| TOC por pagina (movil) | Funciona: drawer inferior via  |
| Busqueda Ctrl+K | Funciona: busqueda estatica de titulos via  |
| Anchor links de headings | Funcionan: IDs generados via  en  |
| Boton copiar en bloques de codigo | Funciona via  |
| Sidebar movil | Funciona via  |
| View transitions | Funcionan: CSS view transitions en navegacion |

Como deben actualizar docs futuros agentes

1. Anadir un endpoint backend: actualizar . Anadir ejemplos request/response. Verificar desde los  y  reales. Marcar lo no verificado con .
2. Anadir una pagina o hook frontend: actualizar .
3. Anadir una seccion o pagina de docs: crear/actualizar el contenido en los tres arboles de locale (,  y ) en el mismo cambio. Si la traduccion completa no esta terminada, anadir heading traducido y marcar la pagina como .
4. Mantener alineada la profundidad por locale: cuando EN reciba una pagina hija mas profunda, anadir placeholders o traducciones equivalentes en ES/EUS para que el conteo de paginas y la estructura de nav no diverjan silenciosamente.
5. Arreglar un link roto: todos los links internos deben llevar prefijo de locale (por ejemplo ). Los links sin locale tambien funcionan via redirect de middleware, pero se prefieren links con locale explicito.
6. Mantener docs veraces: no documentar comportamiento que no pueda verificarse desde el codigo. Usar callouts  con "Needs verification" para cualquier cosa incierta.

Patrón de validación DTO

Los módulos DTO validan las entradas de query/body antes de ejecutar el servicio y lanzan  cuando son inválidas. Los manejadores de ruta centralizan los errores mediante  en .

Patrones de servicio y repositorio

- Los servicios coordinan reglas, transiciones y efectos secundarios.
- Los repositorios encapsulan consultas SQL y devuelven objetos mapeados del dominio.
- El comportamiento compartido entre dominios se extrae (por ejemplo, efectos secundarios del estado del paquete).

Manejo de errores y helpers de respuesta

- Errores personalizados: , , , , .
-  mapea errores a estado HTTP y una forma consistente .
-  permite establecer/eliminar cookies.

Autenticación y autorización

- Token de acceso: JWT Bearer requerido para endpoints protegidos.
-  verifica el token y restricciones de rol opcionales.
- Los roles se definen en  como  y .

Conexión MySQL

 construye y almacena en caché de forma perezosa un pool con límite de 10 conexiones. SSL está habilitado con  en la implementación actual.

Envío de emails

El cliente Resend se usa para:

- activación de cuentas,
- restablecimiento de contraseña,
- notificaciones de inicio de sesión,
- emails de estado de paquetes (pending/assigned/in_transit/delivered/undelivered/failed).

Integración con Google Directions

:

- llama a Google Directions con optimización de waypoints,
- calcula paradas ordenadas + ETA,
- devuelve duración y distancia totales.

 convierte una dirección textual en coordenadas para los flujos de creación/actualización de paquetes.

Efectos secundarios del estado del paquete

 realiza:

1. inserción de log en ,
2. búsqueda del token de seguimiento,
3. búsqueda del nombre del distribuidor,
4. envío del email de estado al destinatario.

Enlace de seguimiento

Los tokens de seguimiento se almacenan en la tabla  con tipo , vinculados al paquete, y utilizados por el endpoint público:

- 

Flujo de restablecimiento de contraseña

1.  crea/revoca el token de reset y envía el email.
2. :
   - solo con token: comprobación de validación,

Flujo de activación de cuenta

La creación de usuario dispara la generación del email de activación mediante . El tipo de token de activación es  y es validado por el mismo mecanismo de verificación de tokens de .

> [!WARNING]

Logs
